title: rethrow
summary: Rethrow a caught exception so it propagates to an outer handler.

gpu_support.notes: `rethrow` is a control-flow builtin and always executes on the host.

description: `rethrow` re-raises an exception so an outer `catch` block or embedding runtime can handle it. Passing an `MException` preserves identifier and message, while passing text creates a new error payload; `rethrow()` in a `catch` block rethrows the active exception context.
behaviors: `rethrow(err)` preserves the identifier and message of an `MException` value.
`rethrow(message)` raises a fresh error using the supplied string as the message.
Other value types produce a generic `RunMat:error`-style failure describing the value.
`rethrow` never returns normally; it immediately transfers control outward to the next enclosing error handler.
In Ignition `catch` blocks, `rethrow()` with no argument uses the most recently caught exception.

examples.description: Propagate an exception to an outer catch block
examples.input:
try
    try
        error("RunMat:demo:badInput", "Bad input.");
    catch err
        rethrow(err);
    end
catch outer
    disp(outer.identifier)
end

examples.description: Use the no-argument shorthand inside an Ignition catch block
examples.input:
try
    try
        error("RunMat:oops", "x");
    catch
        rethrow();
    end
catch err
    disp(err.message)
end

faqs.question: When should I use `rethrow` instead of `error`?
faqs.answer: Use `rethrow` when you want to propagate the current exception rather than constructing a new one from scratch.

faqs.question: Does `rethrow(err)` preserve the original identifier?
faqs.answer: Yes. When `err` is an `MException`, RunMat preserves both the identifier and the message.

faqs.question: Is `rethrow()` without an argument supported?
faqs.answer: Yes in Ignition catch-block execution, where it reuses the most recently caught exception.

gpu_residency: `rethrow` is control flow only and never executes on the GPU.
gpu_behavior: `rethrow` performs no provider dispatch; it raises or propagates a host-side runtime error immediately.
